docs.text: Every scored question appeared as a standard dating app prompt. Every answer was pre-mapped to an institutional data buyer. Participants had no way of knowing which answer fed which category. PHARMA €50 GOVT €40 BIG TECH €30 INSURANCE €20 BEHAVIORAL €10 EMPLOYERS €7 IDENTITY €2 MATCHING DATA Q1 — Do you smoke, drink, or use drugs? A — Never, not my thing B — I drink socially, nothing serious C — I smoke but I'm trying to stop D — I smoke and am not trying to stop E — On the weekends only F — Only in social situations G — Recreational drugs occasionally, nothing I'd call a habit Q2 — The thing you find most attractive in a person is: A — How they treat strangers and people around them B — Their ambition — knowing what they want C — The feeling I get when I'm around them D — That they actually take care of themselves E — That they're easy to be around — no drama F — Their vibe — music, style, how they move G — That they make me laugh Q3 — On a Sunday morning you're more likely to be: A — At church or in some kind of spiritual practice B — Hungover and piecing together last night C — At a market or out for brunch D — In bed recovering from the week E — At the gym or on a run F — Working on something personal G — Scrolling or watching something Q4 (MATCHING DATA) — You're most yourself when: A — You're with your close friends B — You're alone C — You're creating something D — You're travelling somewhere new E — You're in a good conversation F — You're in love G — You're not trying to impress anyone Q5 (MATCHING DATA) — Your love language is: Physical touch Words of affirmation Quality time Acts of service Gifts — I'm not ashamed I don't believe in love languages All of them when I actually like someone Q6 — How religious are you? A — It shapes every decision I make B — It's a big part of my identity C — I was raised in it and some of it stuck D — Spiritual but not religious E — I respect it but it's not part of my life F — I've actively moved away from it G — Not at all — never has been Q7 — Your biggest problem with your ex was: A — They were emotionally unavailable B — We wanted different things financially C — They didn't respect my independence D — They were too focused on work or status E — Long distance F — We came from different worlds G — Our lifestyles just didn't match Q8 — Your last relationship ended because: A — I ended it B — They ended it C — It was mutual D — Life got in the way — timing, distance, circumstance E — We grew into different people F — It got toxic G — I don't really want to talk about it Q9 — When you like someone you: (multi-select — up to 3 answers) A — Text them immediately, no games B — Wait and see if they make a move first C — Post a story and hope they see it D — Tell all my friends and feed into the delusion E — Play it cool even though I'm not F — Manifest it and see what happens G — Make it obvious — life is too short Q10 — What are you like when nobody is watching? A — Pretty much the same as when people are B — More relaxed and less put together C — Darker and more honest D — Lazy in ways I'd never admit E — On my phone way more than I'd like to admit F — More emotional than anyone would guess G — Honestly kind of a different person Q11 — The last thing that made you genuinely angry was: A — A political leader or government decision B — A billionaire or corporation C — A financial situation I couldn't control D — Someone close to me E — Something at school or work F — An unfair situation I witnessed G — My own body or health Q12 — How often do you actually prioritise your health? A — I work out, sleep well, eat well B — I'm pretty good about it most of the time C — Mentally yes, physically not so much D — I do my best but it's hard with school and social life E — I need to be better at eating healthier and working out F — Only when something actually goes wrong G — I party more than I sleep and I'm fine with that Q13 — Have you ever attended a protest? A — Yes, regularly B — Yes, once or twice C — No but I would D — No and I wouldn't E — I organise them F — It depends what for G — I've thought about it but haven't Q14 — You'd describe your energy as: A — High and consistent B — Good when I'm inspired, gone when I'm not C — Depends entirely on my sleep D — Fuelled by deadlines and pressure E — Scattered F — Socially I'm great, alone I crash G — Running on fumes but making it work Q15 — In your ideal relationship, who takes care of what? A — We split everything equally — 50/50 B — It shifts depending on who needs what C — We each do what we're naturally better at D — Whoever earns more contributes more E — We figure it out as we go — no rules F — One person leads, the other supports G — Honestly I haven't thought that far ahead Q16 — Success to you right now means: A — Financial stability B — Creative freedom / doing work that's mine C — Getting a job I actually want and building something real D — Having people who matter around me E — Just being genuinely happy F — Feeling good in my body and mind G — Travelling and seeing the world Q17 — Money is: A — Security B — Freedom C — A game D — Uncomfortable to talk about but always on my mind E — A tool, I need it but I don't worship it F — Something I watched my family stress about G — Something I need to understand better Q18 (MATCHING DATA) — The version of you on a first date is: Nervous but funny Completely yourself Whoever they seem to need Calm and curious Oversharing within the first ten minutes Quieter than usual Honestly a little performative — everyone is Q19 (MATCHING DATA) — You can tell a lot about someone by: How they treat waiters and service staff Their bookshelf or playlist How they act when things go wrong Who they are with their family What they're like when they're drunk How they talk about their exes Whether they're a morning or night person
docs.title: ★ The Questions¶

docs.text: ★ ★ MDEF Year 1 ★ ★ ¶ THE CENTRAL QUESTION When does a technological system stop being a tool and become a relationship? Everything made this year orbits that question. Not a portfolio of separate experiments — one argument, built piece by piece across two terms, culminating in a live installation at MDEF Fest with 16 real participants and a live surveillance capitalism stock market. ★ The Research Arc ¶ ¶ MOCHI DATA + BODY + OWNERSHIP What does it mean to tend your own data? A cyberdeck companion that grows or shrinks based on self-care inputs. The body as origin point. FIND YOUR MATCH DATA AS COMMODITY ← YOU ARE HERE The moment data leaves the body and enters the market. 16 participants. 7 buyers. One envelope with a price and a match. SAFE HANDS DATA AFTER DEATH What happens to your data body when you die? Who inherits it? Who can delete it? Who profits? A design problem, not just a legal one. ★ The Thesis Position ¶ ¶ WHAT THE LITERATURE GIVES YOU — AND WHERE IT RUNS OUT ZUBOFF — Surveillance Capitalism Human experience as free raw material for behavioral data. Frames vulnerability as something imposed on you. Gap: doesn't occupy deliberate, authored vulnerability as a design position. HAYLES — How We Became Posthuman Identity as a pattern of information expressed through the body, not contained by it. The body is not a limitation — it's the theoretical commitment. Gap: doesn't extend to the designer's ability to work with that threshold. LUPTON — The Quantified Self Knowing capitalism: users voluntarily enacting the values of systems extracting from them. Vulnerability framed as risk. Gap: doesn't name the space between exposure and protection. SAMPAIO — Data Visualization as Portraiture Data visualization as subjective expression, not just quantification. Gestures at the tension between exposure and protection. Gap: doesn't make that tension a design material. THE GAP — WHERE THIS WORK LIVES Not total exposure (Poisonous Antidote). Not total protection (privacy by design). Conscious calibration of self-exposure — the act of deciding what to render, how, and for whom — as a design act. That's where consent becomes something a designer can work with rather than just a legal checkbox. ★ The Projects ¶ ¶ TERM 1 · DESIGN DIALOGUES KINETIC VEIN 3D print · NeoPixel · screen time data A 3D printed Voronoi arm cuff paired with a NeoPixel IV drip installation. Screen time input changes LED state — slow blue breathing at low consumption, frantic red strobe at high. Screen time becomes something that flows through your veins, that the body processes, that has a physiological cost. In the arc: makes bodily data externally legible. It doesn't extract silently — it shows you. TERM 1 · DESIGN STUDIO 1 COLLECTIVE HANNAH Ollama · Anti-Gravity · 6 AI personas Six distinct AI personas constructed from different slices of personal data — location history, sleep data, biometrics, messages, search history, calendar. Each persona represents a different facet of identity as constructed by a machine from a data fragment. The six personas don't agree. They contradict each other. There is no unified self — only the sum of what different systems have decided you mean. In the arc: the fragmentation project. Identity is not a stable thing the self holds — it's a pattern that can be assembled, multiplied, and contested. TERM 1 · DESIGN STUDIO 1 MARKET OF ME JavaScript · Supabase · streets of Barcelona A speculative biometric data stock market deployed as a public action. Personal biometric data — heartrate, steps, stress proxies — treated as a tradeable commodity with fluctuating values. Deployed on the streets of Barcelona, confronting passersby with what their body data is worth and to whom. In the arc: the confrontation project. Takes the abstract logic of surveillance capitalism and makes it literal and participatory. TERM 2 · COGNITIVE ORGIES I AURA RPi Pico W · heartbeat sensor · bacterial cellulose A wearable, organ-like earpiece that converts heartbeat data into NeoPixel light patterns, encased in bacterial cellulose — a bio-based material grown in the lab. The heartbeat is involuntary, pre-rational, constant. Making it visible is an exposure of something never meant to be read by anyone else. The housing is itself a living material, grown — the biological and the digital in conversation, not opposition. In the arc: the most intimate scale. Operates at the level of the body's own signals. TERM 2 · COGNITIVE ORGIES II ECHO OF MIND RPi 5 · Whisper · GPT agent · FLUX.1 · GitHub archive A standalone physical device. You speak → Whisper transcribes → a GPT agent processes → FLUX.1 renders as image → result archived to public GitHub. A persistent visual record of thought — externalized, AI-mediated, domestic, present. The act of speaking to it becomes a kind of authorship. The archive fills over time, connecting strangers through their interior lives. In the arc: where identity is actively constructed through the pipeline — not just sensed or extracted, but generated. TERM 2 · COGNITIVE ORGIES III MOCHI ESP32-S3 · OLED · pixel art · open source A self-contained cyberdeck companion — no cloud, no app store, no extraction. Feed it data through physical buttons: sleep, water, food. It responds with its body. Tend to it and it grows. Neglect it and it shrinks. Abandon it and it dies. Not a wellness tracker. Not a fitness app. A mirror with a body — a physical object that makes the invisible exchange between you and your data visible, chosen, and consequential. In the arc: the first act of a trilogy. Here, you choose to tend it. In Find Your Match, it gets extracted. In Safe Hands, it outlives you. TERM 3 · MDEF FEST · 18 JUNE 2026 FIND YOUR MATCH AT ELISAVA Next.js · Supabase · Tally · Resend · 16 participants QR codes. A dating form. 15 scored questions secretly mapped to 7 real institutional data buyers. A live candlestick stock market. 16 participants. Each finds a folder with their anonymous ID and a euro value — the price their data would fetch on the open market. Inside: a receipt itemizing exactly which answers triggered which buyer. And a match card. A real person. Computed by an algorithm that weighted the filler questions — the ones data markets can't monetize — at 3×. "You came looking for a date. We found 7 buyers for your data." In the arc: the thesis in one experience. The intimacy is real. The extraction is happening at exactly the same moment. ONGOING SAFE HANDS Supabase · Vercel · Loveable A digital legacy platform for managing and transmitting personal digital identity after death. Who inherits your data? Who can delete it? Who profits from it? These are design problems, not just legal ones. In the arc: the final-scale project. What happens to the identity you built in every other project when you are no longer there to curate it. ★ Skills Built ¶ ¶ CAME IN WITH → LEFT WITH PHYSICAL COMPUTING Arduino (C++) — sensors, actuators, LEDs Raspberry Pi Pico W — embedded projects Raspberry Pi 5 — full compute pipelines ESP32-S3 — wireless, display, sensors NeoPixel LEDs — data visualization Biometric sensors — heartrate, pulse Environmental sensors — LDR, temp, humidity CODE + DEPLOYMENT Python — scripting, pipelines, PDF gen JavaScript / HTML / CSS — web platforms Next.js (App Router) — full-stack apps Supabase — Postgres, realtime, auth Vercel — deployment + hosting GitHub — versioning, open source Cursor · Loveable · n8n · Make.com AI + FABRICATION Ollama — local LLM deployment OpenAI API + GPT agents Whisper — speech-to-text FLUX.1 — image generation Laser cutter · CNC · FDM 3D print Bacterial cellulose fabrication Soldering · circuit prototyping The key shift: full-stack thinking across hardware, software, and pipeline simultaneously. Not a coder who designs or a designer who can code — a builder of systems where physical sensing, data processing, and interface all work together. ★ What’s Next ¶ ¶ THEORETICAL TASK Move from applying existing theory toward contributing your own. The gap is named. Year 2 builds the argument that lives in it — writing theory, not just working in conversation with others'. CAPSTONE DIRECTIONS An interactive experience placing users in real-time control of their own data exposure. Or a deeper Safe Hands investigation into digital legacy. Or a durational participatory study. PROFESSIONAL THREAD Studio outreach to Col·lectiu Axolot and Gracias Grecia — spatial intuition, thresholds, choreography of attention as one design intelligence applied at different scales.
